title: Implement Stack Using Array
description: Implement a stack using an array. Process q operations where 1 x means push x, 2 means pop, and 3 means print the top element or -1 if the stack is empty.
inputFormat: q lines of queries in the format 1 x, 2, or 3
outputFormat: For each type 3 query, print the current top or -1
intendedApproachSummary: Maintain an array and a top pointer. Push increments top, pop decrements top, and peek reads arr[top].
wrongApproachHints: Do not shift array elements on pop.
Check empty state before pop or top queries.
hints: Initialize top = -1.
Push writes to ++top, pop reads from top--.

title: Balanced Brackets
description: Given a string containing only (), {}, and [], print Balanced if the brackets are valid, otherwise print Not Balanced.
edgeCases: Open brackets remain at the end
intendedApproachSummary: Push opening brackets. On each closing bracket, the top must be its matching opener.
wrongApproachHints: Counting only the number of brackets is not enough.
You must also check that the stack is empty at the end.
hints: Map each closing bracket to its expected opener.
Check isEmpty before pop.

examples.explanation: Each closing bracket matches the most recent unmatched opener.

title: Reverse a Word Using Stack
description: Given a lowercase string, reverse it using a stack and print the reversed string.
intendedApproachSummary: Push every character, then pop until the stack becomes empty.
wrongApproachHints: This problem is about using the stack explicitly, not just scanning backward.
Remember that popping already reverses order.
hints: Push characters first.
Build the answer while popping.

examples.explanation: The last pushed character comes out first.

title: Remove Adjacent Duplicates
description: Repeatedly remove adjacent equal characters from a string until no such pair remains. Print the final string.
outputFormat: Print the reduced string or Empty if nothing remains
intendedApproachSummary: Use a stack of characters. If the current character equals the top, pop it; otherwise push it.
wrongApproachHints: Avoid repeated string deletion inside loops.
Remember that removals can expose new adjacent duplicates.
hints: Compare the current character with the stack top.
Build the final string from the remaining stack.

examples.explanation: bb is removed first, then aa is removed.

title: Evaluate Postfix Expression
description: Given a postfix expression with single-digit operands and operators +, -, *, /, evaluate and print the result.
inputFormat: A postfix expression string without spaces
edgeCases: Division should use a / b where a was pushed before b
intendedApproachSummary: Push operands. On an operator, pop b first, then a, compute a op b, and push the result back.
wrongApproachHints: Do not pop operands in reverse order for subtraction and division.
Every operator reduces two stack elements into one.
hints: Digits are operands.
Pop right operand before left operand.

title: Evaluate Prefix Expression
description: Given a prefix expression with single-digit operands and operators +, -, *, /, evaluate and print the result.
inputFormat: A prefix expression string without spaces
edgeCases: Subtraction and division order still matter
intendedApproachSummary: Scan from right to left. Push operands, and on an operator pop the left then right logical values in the correct order.
wrongApproachHints: Scanning left to right makes the stack logic awkward here.
Keep operand order correct for non-commutative operators.
hints: Reverse the traversal direction.
After popping two values, combine them and push back.

title: Min Stack Queries
description: Process q queries on a stack: 1 x push x, 2 pop, 3 print current minimum, or -1 if empty. Every operation must support O(1) minimum retrieval.
outputFormat: For each type 3 query, print the minimum or -1
intendedApproachSummary: Track a second stack of current minimum values or store the minimum with each push.
wrongApproachHints: Do not scan the whole stack to answer minimum queries.
Be careful when the current minimum is popped.
hints: Push Math.min(x, currentMin) onto the min stack.
Pop from both stacks together.

examples.explanation: The auxiliary minimum stack keeps the current minimum at each depth.

title: Stock Span
description: For each day's stock price, print how many consecutive days ending today had price less than or equal to today's price.
intendedApproachSummary: Maintain a decreasing stack of indices. Pop smaller or equal prices before computing the current span.
wrongApproachHints: Nested left scans are too slow.
Indices are more useful than raw values for the span formula.
hints: If the stack becomes empty, the span is i + 1.
Otherwise span = i - previousGreaterIndex.

examples.explanation: Each span stretches back until a higher price blocks it.

title: Next Greater Element to the Right
description: For each array element, print the nearest greater element to its right, or -1 if none exists.
intendedApproachSummary: Traverse from right to left while maintaining a decreasing stack of candidates.
wrongApproachHints: A nested search to the right is too slow.
Pop values that cannot serve as a greater answer.
hints: Process from the end.
While top <= current, pop it.

examples.explanation: Each answer is the first larger value to the right.

title: Previous Smaller Element
description: For each element, print the nearest smaller element on its left, or -1 if it does not exist.
intendedApproachSummary: Traverse from left to right while keeping an increasing stack of candidates.
wrongApproachHints: You must remove larger or equal elements before answering.
A stack of candidates is enough; nested loops are not.
hints: While top >= current, pop it.
After answering, push the current value.

examples.explanation: Each value looks to the closest smaller value on the left.

title: Infix to Postfix
description: Convert an infix expression containing operands, +, -, *, /, ^, and parentheses into postfix form.
inputFormat: A valid infix expression string without spaces
intendedApproachSummary: Send operands directly to output. Use a stack for operators and pop while precedence rules allow.
wrongApproachHints: You cannot just remove parentheses and keep the same order.
Handle precedence and parentheses separately.
hints: On ')', pop until '(' is found.
Pop higher-precedence operators before pushing the new one.

examples.explanation: Multiplication and the parenthesized subtraction must stay together before the final addition.

title: Daily Temperatures
description: For each day, print how many days you would have to wait until a warmer temperature. If there is no future warmer day, print 0.
intendedApproachSummary: Use a decreasing stack of indices. When a warmer temperature appears, pop colder indices and compute the wait.
wrongApproachHints: Do not scan rightward from every day.
Store indices so you can compute day differences.
hints: While current temperature is greater than the temperature at the top index, resolve that index.

examples.explanation: Each day waits until the first warmer future day.

title: Circular Next Greater Element
description: For each element in a circular array, print the next greater element encountered while moving right cyclically. Print -1 if none exists.
intendedApproachSummary: Simulate a doubled traversal from right to left with modulo indexing while maintaining a decreasing stack.
wrongApproachHints: Do not actually build a doubled array if you do not need to.
Only one extra wrap is required.
hints: Iterate from 2n - 1 down to 0.
Only record answers during the first n positions.

examples.explanation: The last 1 wraps to the beginning to find 2.

title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
description: Given histogram bar heights, print the maximum rectangle area that can be formed.
intendedApproachSummary: Maintain an increasing stack of indices. When a shorter bar appears, pop heights and compute the area where each popped height is the limiting bar.
wrongApproachHints: Checking every left-right span is too slow.
The width comes from the previous smaller and next smaller boundaries.
hints: Use indices, not just heights.
Run a cleanup pass after the main loop or append a sentinel bar.

examples.explanation: The best rectangle uses heights 5 and 6 with width 2, giving area 10.

title: Maximal Rectangle in Binary Matrix
description: Given a binary matrix, print the area of the largest rectangle containing only 1s.
inputFormat: n lines each containing m binary digits separated by spaces
intendedApproachSummary: Build histogram heights row by row and reuse the largest-rectangle-in-histogram logic on each row.
wrongApproachHints: Checking every rectangle directly is too slow.
Reset histogram height to 0 when a cell contains 0.
hints: Treat each row as the base of a histogram.
Update heights cumulatively.

examples.explanation: The best rectangle covers a 3x2 block of 1s.

title: Validate Stack Sequences
description: Given pushed and popped sequences of distinct values, print Valid if they could describe a stack, otherwise print Invalid.
edgeCases: Entire pop chain after one push sequence
intendedApproachSummary: Simulate the pushes, and after every push pop as long as the stack top matches the next required popped value.
wrongApproachHints: Do not compare positions without simulating the stack state.
One push can trigger multiple pops.
hints: Track the index into the popped array.
Keep popping while the top matches popped[j].

examples.explanation: This pop sequence is achievable after pushing all values.

title: Asteroid Collision
description: Given integers representing asteroids moving right (positive) or left (negative), simulate collisions and print the surviving asteroids.
outputFormat: Print the surviving asteroids in order, or Empty if none survive
edgeCases: Multiple previous asteroids collide with one new asteroid
intendedApproachSummary: Keep surviving asteroids in a stack. Only a right-moving top and a left-moving current asteroid can collide.
wrongApproachHints: Do not try every pair.
One incoming asteroid may destroy several previous ones.
hints: Collisions happen only when stack top > 0 and current < 0.
Use a loop to resolve repeated collisions.

title: Simplify Unix Path
description: Given an absolute Unix path, simplify it by resolving ., .., and extra slashes.
edgeCases: Moving above root should stay at root
intendedApproachSummary: Split by slash and simulate directory navigation with a stack of path segments.
wrongApproachHints: String replacement alone does not handle .. correctly.
Ignore empty tokens and '.' tokens.
hints: Pop on '..' if the stack is not empty.
Join the remaining segments with '/'.

examples.explanation: The path moves into a, then b, then climbs back twice before entering c.
